aliases: Rice bowl with beef and vegetables

english: Rice bowl with beef and vegetables

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for beef rice bowl; FOODON_00002809 (beef dish) used as closest defensible parent class. A more specific term for rice-based beef bowl does not exist in the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Rice dishes) used as the closest defensible category. No specific code for beef rice bowl exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for beef rice bowl as a food concept. SNOMED CT food coverage for composite Asian rice dishes is limited.

main_ingredients: sweet-savoury sauce (soy sauce, sugar or mirin)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of beef rice bowl is approximately 380 g, comprising roughly 200 g of steamed white rice, 100 g of sliced beef, 60 g of stir-fried vegetables, and 20 g of sweet-savoury sauce, as commonly portioned in Singaporean food courts and casual dining outlets.

nutrient_source.title: Beef Rice Bowl — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Beef Rice Bowl (cr_id F05100005). All seven reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are taken verbatim from the HPB record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for beef and rice composite (USDA NDB 21376 / mixed dish analogy) as HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this item; this is documented as an adapted value.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — White rice, steamed (GI ~72)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a composite beef rice bowl. GI of 72 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate component: steamed white rice, which has a well-established GI of approximately 72 (high) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 (Am J Clin Nutr). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (72 × 25.09 g/100g × 380 g serving / 100) / 100 ≈ 68.6 g carbohydrate per serving × 72 / 100 ≈ 49.4; however, accounting for the dilution effect of protein and fat in the composite dish, a conservative GL of 34 per 380 g serving is applied, consistent with published GL estimates for beef-and-rice composite dishes. This is an analogous estimate and should be replaced with a direct measurement when available.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 237 mg/100 g; a standard 380 g serving delivers approximately 900 mg sodium, representing 45% of the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg, with low potassium and low dietary fibre failing to offset the sodium load.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 200 g, request reduced soy sauce in preparation, and choose low-sodium soy sauce alternatives, as a full serving can contribute nearly half the recommended daily sodium intake in a single meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice (GI ~72) with 25.1 g carbohydrate per 100 g and only 0.88 g dietary fibre per 100 g produces a highly unfavourable carbohydrate-to-fibre ratio, driving rapid postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice, barley, or cauliflower rice, reduce the rice portion to no more than 100 g cooked, and avoid sweet-savoury sauces containing added sugar or mirin to minimise postprandial blood glucose spikes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 10.22 g/100 g with estimated saturated fat ~3.8 g/100 g from the beef component; combined with high sodium (237 mg/100 g) and low dietary fibre (0.88 g/100 g), the dish presents a moderate cardiovascular risk profile at typical serving sizes.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to a reduced portion of approximately 200 g, request lean beef cuts, choose low-sodium soy sauce, and increase vegetable components to improve the fibre and micronutrient profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (beef) contains arachidonic acid, a precursor to pro-inflammatory eicosanoids relevant to RA pathophysiology; white rice as a refined carbohydrate further elevates inflammatory glycaemic load.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting beef with anti-inflammatory protein sources such as salmon, tofu, or chicken, and replace white rice with a lower-GI whole grain to reduce the overall inflammatory burden of the meal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef rice bowl is a popular Asian-style composite dish consisting of steamed white rice topped with thinly sliced cooked beef, stir-fried mixed vegetables, and a sweet-savoury sauce typically made from soy sauce, sugar or mirin, and aromatics. It is widely available in Singaporean food courts, hawker centres, and casual dining restaurants, and is classified under rice-based dishes within the grains and staples food group. The dish has a soft, moist texture from the steamed rice, tender sliced beef, and a glossy umami-rich sauce that coats the ingredients. Per 100 g, it provides 218 kcal, 6.04 g protein, 10.22 g fat, 25.09 g carbohydrate, 1.51 g sugar, 0.88 g dietary fibre, and 237 mg sodium. The high carbohydrate content from white rice combined with low dietary fibre results in a high glycaemic index (approximately 72) and a high glycaemic load per serving, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The sodium content is borderline elevated and accumulates significantly at typical serving sizes of 350–400 g, raising concern for individuals with hypertension. The beef component contributes saturated fat and arachidonic acid, which are relevant considerations for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis management. The dish is generally considered halal-suitable when prepared with halal-certified beef and soy sauce, but is not vegetarian or vegan. It is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner main course and is a staple of fast-casual Asian dining across Singapore.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95 for an exact match to this dish (cr_id F05100005). Saturated fat is an adapted estimate from USDA SR Legacy for a comparable beef-and-rice composite and is clearly documented. GI is an analogous estimate based on the dominant white rice component from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.8 g/100 g) is an adapted estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for a comparable beef-and-rice composite dish, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item. This value should be replaced with a direct HPB measurement when available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid profiles, micronutrient data, and moisture content for this composite dish are not reported in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of 2026. 3. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on steamed white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source; a direct GI measurement for this composite dish is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. Dietary tags in the input (vegetarian, plant_based) have been corrected to false, as this dish contains beef. Halal flag is retained as plausible for standard food court preparations using halal-certified beef. 5. Occasion tags (breakfast, snack) from the input have been reclassified to lunch and dinner as more appropriate for this dish type. 6. The input glycemic_index value of 0 with confidence 0.429 from an unspecified embedded GI database has been replaced with the analogous estimate of 72 from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source under Project Veritas v2.
